subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 11! In this unit you will explore the geometry of turning and the geometry of circles. Every time you turn a corner on the way to the masjid, spin around to face a friend, or watch the hands of a clock sweep past the prayer times, you are seeing geometry in action. This unit takes those everyday movements and gives them clear mathematical names, so that you can describe them, predict them, and draw them.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 2 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, and where the text describes a picture, draw that picture yourself on paper or make the turn with your own body. Geometry is a subject you learn best by moving and making, not only by reading. Your AI tutor is ready day and night to give you extra practice, describe a shape in a new way, or check your drawings. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

Why does this matter so much? Turns and angles are the language of direction and design. A builder in Hargeisa uses right angles to make walls that stand up straight. A traveller between Mogadishu and Kismaayo follows turns to stay on the right road. A carpenter checks square corners so that a door will close properly. And circles are everywhere too – the wheel of a cart, the rim of a cooking pot, the round anjero on the plate, the full moon that marks the start of a new month. When you understand these shapes, you understand the world around you a little better.

As you work, keep a pencil, some paper, and a square corner of paper beside you. Stand up when the lesson asks you to turn, and turn your whole body – this helps the idea travel from your body into your mind. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Every mathematician, builder and artist you admire once sat where you sit now, learning these very same ideas about turns, angles and circles.
Describe movement using the words left, right and straight ahead.
Understand and use clockwise and anticlockwise to describe turning.
Recognise a quarter turn, a half turn, a three-quarter turn and a full turn.
Know that four quarter turns make one full turn.
Identify a right angle as the angle made by a quarter turn, and find right angles around you.
Recognise a straight angle as the angle made by a half turn.
Compare angles and say which is greater and which is smaller than a right angle.

concepts.title: Direction – Left, Right and Straight Ahead
concepts.explanation: Before we can talk about turns, we need clear words for direction. The three most useful are left, right and straight ahead. When you walk forward without turning, you go straight ahead. When you change to face the side where your writing hand is (for most people the right hand), you turn right. When you face the other side, you turn left. Directions depend on which way you are facing. This is very important. If Amina faces the masjid and turns right, she is not pointing the same way as Yusuf, who is facing the…
concepts.example: Let us practise reading a path. Suppose Halima starts at her front door facing the road. She walks straight ahead, turns right at the shop, walks straight, then turns left at the school, and the masjid is straight…

concepts.title: Clockwise and Anticlockwise
concepts.explanation: There are two ways to turn on the spot, and mathematics gives each one a name. Look at a clock. The hands always sweep from the 12 towards the 3, then down to the 6, across to the 9, and back to the 12. This direction of turning is called clockwise, because it is the way a clock’s hands move. Turning the other way – from the 12 towards the 9, down to the 6, across to the 3 – is called anticlockwise, which simply means ‘against the clock’. You can feel these turns with your own body. Stand up and face forward. Now…
concepts.example: Why do we need two words instead of just saying ‘turn’? Because the direction of the turn changes where you end up and what you are doing. If you tell a friend only “turn the tap,” they might turn it the wrong way and…

concepts.title: Quarter Turns and the Right Angle
concepts.explanation: Now we measure how much we turn. The most important amount of turn is the quarter turn. A quarter turn is one fourth of a complete spin. Picture a clock again: when the minute hand travels from the 12 all the way round to the 3, it has made a quarter turn. If Aisha stands facing the door and makes a quarter turn clockwise, she ends up facing the wall that was on her right. A quarter turn creates a very special angle called a right angle. A right angle is the ‘square corner’ that you see everywhere: the corner of a…
concepts.example: You can make your own right-angle checker, and it is one of the most useful tools in geometry. Take any square or rectangle of paper – the corner of an exercise book works too. Fold it once, then fold it again so the…

concepts.title: Half Turns and Straight Angles
concepts.explanation: A half turn is two quarter turns joined together. When you make a half turn, you end up facing the opposite direction to where you started. If Farah faces the road and makes a half turn, she now has her back to the road and faces her house. On a clock, a half turn takes the minute hand from the 12 all the way round to the 6 – halfway round the whole clock. It helps to see how the turns build on each other. One quarter turn makes a right angle – a square corner. Two quarter turns make a half turn – a straight…
concepts.example: Predicting the result of a half turn is a great way to train your spatial thinking. Suppose an arrow points up. After a half turn, which way does it point? It points down, the opposite direction. If an arrow points left…

concepts.title: Full Turns, Three-Quarter Turns and Comparing Angles
concepts.explanation: A full turn is a complete spin that brings you right back to where you started, facing the very same way. A full turn is made of four quarter turns, or of two half turns – both add up to the same complete spin. On a clock, the minute hand makes a full turn when it travels all the way from the 12 round to the 12 again, which takes one whole hour. In between a half turn and a full turn is the three-quarter turn, which is three quarter turns joined together. If Maryam faces the door and makes a three-quarter turn…
concepts.example: We can also compare angles by how much turn they hold. The right angle is our measuring stick. An angle smaller than a right angle is a narrow, sharp corner – like the pointed tip of a slice of mango, or a slightly…

concepts.title: The Circle and Its Centre
concepts.explanation: Now we turn to a shape that is all about turning: the circle. A circle is a perfectly round, flat shape with no corners and no straight sides. You see circles everywhere – the rim of a cooking pot, the wheel of a cart, a coin, a plate of bariis, the full moon. What makes a shape a true circle is not just that it looks round; it is a special rule about distance. Every circle has a middle point called the centre. The special rule is this: every point on the edge of a circle is exactly the same distance from the…
concepts.example: You can find the centre of a paper circle by folding. Take a circle cut from paper and fold it exactly in half so the two halves match. Open it, then fold it in half again a different way, so the two halves match once…

explorations.prompt: How many quarter turns are in one full turn?
explorations.hint: Represent the information, name the rule, then solve one step at a time.

methods.title: How to check for a right angle
methods.example: The minute hand moves from the 12 to the 6. What turn is this, and what angle does it make?
steps: Fold a sheet of paper once, then fold it again so the folded edges lie on top of each other.
The corner where the folds meet is a perfect right angle.
Hold this corner against the angle you want to test.
If it fits exactly, the angle is a right angle. If not, it is smaller or bigger.

methods.title: How to name a turn
methods.example: Hassan faces the door. He makes a quarter turn clockwise three times. Which way does he face now?
steps: Look at where you start and where you finish facing.
One quarter of the way round is a quarter turn (right angle).
Halfway round is a half turn (straight angle).
All the way round, back to the start, is a full turn.

methods.title: How to find the centre of a circle
methods.example: Aisha holds her folded-paper corner against the corner of a book. It fits exactly. What does this tell her?
steps: Fold the circle in half so the edges match; open it.
Fold it in half again a different way; open it.
The two fold lines cross at the centre.

methods.title: Comparing an angle to a right angle
methods.example: A pair of scissors is opened just a little way. Is the angle between the blades smaller than, equal to, or bigger than a right angle?
steps: Solution: Only slightly open means a narrow, sharp angle, so it is smaller than a right angle.
If you opened the scissors until the corner looked square, the angle would be exactly a right angle; opened wider still, it would be bigger than a right angle.
Check the comparing an angle to a right angle result carefully.

methods.title: Direction of turning
methods.example: Omar twists a jar lid the same way the hands of a clock move. Is he turning clockwise or anticlockwise?
steps: Solution: The hands of a clock move clockwise, so Omar is turning the lid clockwise.
To loosen the lid he would turn it the other way, which is anticlockwise.
Check the direction of turning result carefully.

methods.title: Finding the centre of a circle
methods.example: Sahra has a paper circle. How can she find its centre by folding, and why does it work?
steps: Solution: She folds the circle in half so the halves match, then unfolds and folds in half again a different way.
The two fold lines cross at the centre.
It works because every fold through the centre is a line of symmetry, and the only point shared by two different such lines is the centre itself.

workedExamples.title: Naming a turn on a clock
workedExamples.prompt: The minute hand moves from the 12 to the 6. What turn is this, and what angle does it make?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: From 12 to 6 is halfway round the clock, so it is a half turn. A half turn makes a straight angle, which looks like a straight line. It is the same as two quarter turns joined together.

workedExamples.title: Counting quarter turns
workedExamples.prompt: Hassan faces the door. He makes a quarter turn clockwise three times. Which way does he face now?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: One quarter turn – he faces the right wall. Two quarter turns – he faces the back wall. Three quarter turns – he faces the left wall. Three quarter turns is a three-quarter turn altogether.

workedExamples.title: Testing a right angle
workedExamples.prompt: Aisha holds her folded-paper corner against the corner of a book. It fits exactly. What does this tell her?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Because the paper corner fits the book’s corner exactly, the book’s corner is a right angle – a perfect square corner. The paper corner is a right-angle checker made by folding a sheet twice.

workedExamples.title: Comparing an angle to a right angle
workedExamples.prompt: A pair of scissors is opened just a little way. Is the angle between the blades smaller than, equal to, or bigger than a right angle?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Only slightly open means a narrow, sharp angle, so it is smaller than a right angle. If you opened the scissors until the corner looked square, the angle would be exactly a right angle; opened wider still, it would be bigger than a right angle.

workedExamples.title: Direction of turning
workedExamples.prompt: Omar twists a jar lid the same way the hands of a clock move. Is he turning clockwise or anticlockwise?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The hands of a clock move clockwise, so Omar is turning the lid clockwise. To loosen the lid he would turn it the other way, which is anticlockwise.

workedExamples.title: Finding the centre of a circle
workedExamples.prompt: Sahra has a paper circle. How can she find its centre by folding, and why does it work?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: She folds the circle in half so the halves match, then unfolds and folds in half again a different way. The two fold lines cross at the centre. It works because every fold through the centre is a line of symmetry, and the only point shared by two different such lines is the centre itself.

workedExamples.title: Sharing a circle equally
workedExamples.prompt: Khadija wants to cut a round anjero into four equal pieces. What angles should the cuts make at the centre?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: She draws two lines through the centre that cross at right angles. This makes four right angles meeting at the centre, cutting the anjero into four equal quarters. Four right angles fit exactly around the centre point, which is one full turn.

workedExamples.title: Combining turns
workedExamples.prompt: Maryam makes a half turn, then a quarter turn, both clockwise. How much has she turned altogether, and how does this compare with a full turn?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A half turn is two quarter turns. Adding one more quarter turn gives three quarter turns in total – a three-quarter turn. A full turn is four quarter turns, so Maryam is one quarter turn short of a full turn.

workedExamples.title: Practice 9
workedExamples.prompt: Sort these into smaller than, equal to, or bigger than a right angle: a) a wide-open door b) a square corner c) a nearly-closed pair of scissors.
workedExamples.solution: a) bigger than a right angle b) equal to a right angle c) smaller than a right angle.

workedExamples.title: How many lines of symmetry does a circle have
workedExamples.prompt: How many lines of symmetry does a circle have?

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: Where must a fold line pass so that a circle folds into two matching halves?

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Farah faces the masjid and makes a three-quarter turn anticlockwise. Which way does she face now? Explain with quarter turns.
workedExamples.solution: 1) She faces the wall to her left; a three-quarter turn anticlockwise passes the left wall, the back wall, then stops facing the right-hand side relative to her start – accept an answer showing three quarter turns counted the anticlockwise way.

activities.title: Make a Right-Angle Checker
steps: Take any sheet of paper, even one with rough edges.
Fold it once, anywhere.
Fold it again so the folded edge lies exactly on top of itself.
The corner where the two folds meet is a perfect right angle – your very own checker.
Use it to test corners around your home: books, doors, windows, tables. Which are right angles?

activities.title: Turn With Your Body
steps: Stand and face a wall. Call it your ‘start’.
Make a quarter turn clockwise. Name the new wall you face.
Keep making quarter turns until you are back at the start. How many did you make?
Now try a half turn, then a three-quarter turn, then a full turn, saying each name out loud.
This activity puts the turns into your body so you never forget them.

activities.title: The Angle Hunt Game
activities.materials: your right-angle checker and a pencil and paper.
steps: Walk around your home with your right-angle checker.
Find five corners that are right angles and write down what they are.
Find one corner smaller than a right angle and one bigger than a right angle.
Compare your list with a family member’s list. Did you find the same corners?
This game trains your eyes to spot right angles quickly.

activities.title: Draw and Fold a Circle Investigation
activities.materials: paper, a round object to trace (plate, cup or lid), and scissors if you have them.
steps: Trace around a round object to draw a circle, then cut it out.
Fold the circle exactly in half so the edges match. Open it and look at the fold line.
Fold it in half again a different way. Open it. Where do the two fold lines cross?
Mark that crossing point – it is the centre. Fold through the centre several more ways and watch the halves always match.
You have discovered why a circle has many lines of symmetry – every fold through the centre works.

activities.title: The Clock-Turn Game (Family Game)
activities.materials: a clock or a drawing of a clock face, and your family.
steps: One player calls a start and an end, like ‘from 12 to 6’.
The others say what turn it is (quarter, half, or three-quarter) and which direction.
Take turns being the caller.
Make it harder: call two turns in a row and ask where the hand ends up.
This game links clocks, turns and angles all together.

activities.title: Circle Art With Symmetry
steps: Fold a paper circle through the centre to make a crease.
Draw a small pattern on one half of the crease.
Fold along the crease and imagine the pattern reflected – then draw the matching pattern on the other half.
Open it up. Your design is symmetrical, just like the circle itself!

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand turns and angles best? What was the trickiest part? Where in your home did you find the most right angles? Share your answer with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Turns Rule
rules.text: A quarter turn makes a right angle (a square corner). A half turn makes a straight angle (a straight line). Four quarter turns – or two half turns – make one full turn.

rules.title: Direction Rule
rules.text: Clockwise is the way a clock’s hands move: 12 to 3 to 6 to 9. Anticlockwise is the opposite way.

rules.title: Circle Rule
rules.text: Every point on a circle is the same distance from the centre. Any line through the centre is a line of symmetry, so a circle has many.

terms: A movement that changes the direction you face
The way clock hands move / the opposite way
One fourth of a full turn; makes a right angle
Two quarter turns; makes a straight angle
A complete turn back to the start; four quarter turns
A square corner, made by a quarter turn
A flat, straight-line angle, made by a half turn
A round shape / its equal-distance middle point
A fold line where two halves match exactly
commonMistakes: Picture 12 to 3 to 6 to 9 – that is clockwise
Saying a straight angle is not an angle
A half turn makes a straight angle; it is the amount of turn
Thinking long lines make a bigger angle
Only the amount of turn between the lines matters
Fold through the centre so the halves match

realProblems.prompt: Amina gives directions from the well to her home: straight ahead, turn left at the acacia tree, straight ahead, turn right at the shop, and home is straight ahead. Someone walking back from her home to the well reverses the journey – what is the first turn they make?
realProblems.answer: 1) The first turn on the way back is a turn at the shop – they turn left (reversing the original right turn there).
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) The first turn on the way back is a turn at the shop – they turn left (reversing the original right turn there).

realProblems.prompt: During Eid, Khadija cuts a round cake into four equal pieces. What angle does each cut make at the centre, and what is that angle called?
realProblems.answer: Each cut makes a right angle at the centre; the angle is a right angle.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Each cut makes a right angle at the centre; the angle is a right angle.

realProblems.prompt: Yusuf watches the minute hand of a clock move from the 12 to the 3. What turn is this, what angle does it make, and which direction is it turning?
realProblems.answer: From 12 to 3 is a quarter turn, it makes a right angle, and it is turning clockwise.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: From 12 to 3 is a quarter turn, it makes a right angle, and it is turning clockwise.

realProblems.prompt: Halima traces a lid to draw a circle, then folds it twice through the middle. Why do the two fold lines cross at the centre?
realProblems.answer: Because every fold through the centre is a line of symmetry, and the only point shared by both fold lines is the centre.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Because every fold through the centre is a line of symmetry, and the only point shared by both fold lines is the centre.

realProblems.prompt: Ibrahim opens a pair of scissors slowly. Describe how the angle between the blades changes from when they are nearly closed to when they lie in a straight line.
realProblems.answer: The angle starts very small (smaller than a right angle), grows through a right angle when the blades make a square corner, keeps growing bigger than a right angle, and finally becomes a straight angle when the blades lie in a straight line.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The angle starts very small (smaller than a right angle), grows through a right angle when the blades make a square corner, keeps growing bigger than a right angle, and finally becomes a straight angle when the blades lie in a straight line.

realProblems.prompt: Farah faces the masjid and makes a three-quarter turn anticlockwise. Which way does she face now? Explain with quarter turns.
realProblems.answer: 1) She faces the wall to her left; a three-quarter turn anticlockwise passes the left wall, the back wall, then stops facing the right-hand side relative to her start – accept an answer showing three quarter turns counted the anticlockwise way.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) She faces the wall to her left; a three-quarter turn anticlockwise passes the left wall, the back wall, then stops facing the right-hand side relative to her start – accept an answer showing three quarter turns counted the anticlockwise way.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Farah faces the masjid and makes a three-quarter turn anticlockwise. Which way does she face now? Explain with quarter turns.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1) She faces the wall to her left; a three-quarter turn anticlockwise passes the left wall, the back wall, then stops facing the right-hand side relative to her start – accept an answer showing three quarter turns counted the anticlockwise way.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Two lines cross at the centre of a circle at right angles. Into how many equal parts do they divide the circle, and what is each angle called?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Four equal parts; each angle at the centre is a right angle.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Omar says, You only need long lines to make a big angle. Is this true? Explain what really makes an angle big.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Not true – the length of the lines does not matter; the size of an angle is the amount of turn between the lines.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Draw (or describe) a shape with exactly four right angles and name it.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A square (or rectangle) has exactly four right angles.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Full Turns, Three-Quarter Turns and Comparing Angles.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A full turn is a complete spin that brings you right back to where you started, facing the very same way. A full turn is made of four quarter turns, or of two half turns – both add up to the same complete spin. On a clock, the minute hand makes a full turn when it travels all the way from the 12 round to the 12 again, which takes one whole hour. In between a half turn and a full turn is the three-quarter turn, which is three quarter turns joined together. If Maryam faces the door and makes a three-quarter turn…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in The Circle and Its Centre.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Now we turn to a shape that is all about turning: the circle. A circle is a perfectly round, flat shape with no corners and no straight sides. You see circles everywhere – the rim of a cooking pot, the wheel of a cart, a coin, a plate of bariis, the full moon. What makes a shape a true circle is not just that it looks round; it is a special rule about distance. Every circle has a middle point called the centre. The special rule is this: every point on the edge of a circle is exactly the same distance from the…

questions.question: What does “Turn” mean?
options: A movement that changes the direction you face
The way clock hands move / the opposite way
One fourth of a full turn; makes a right angle
Two quarter turns; makes a straight angle
questions.answer: A movement that changes the direction you face
questions.hint: Use the Unit 11 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Turn means A movement that changes the direction you face.

questions.question: What does “Clockwise / Anticlockwise” mean?
options: The way clock hands move / the opposite way
One fourth of a full turn; makes a right angle
Two quarter turns; makes a straight angle
A complete turn back to the start; four quarter turns
questions.answer: The way clock hands move / the opposite way
questions.hint: Use the Unit 11 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Clockwise / Anticlockwise means The way clock hands move / the opposite way.

questions.question: What does “Quarter turn” mean?
options: One fourth of a full turn; makes a right angle
Two quarter turns; makes a straight angle
A complete turn back to the start; four quarter turns
A square corner, made by a quarter turn
questions.answer: One fourth of a full turn; makes a right angle
questions.hint: Use the Unit 11 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Quarter turn means One fourth of a full turn; makes a right angle.

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, and where the text describes a picture, draw that picture yourself on paper or make the turn with your own body. Geometry is a subject you learn best by moving and making, not only by reading. Your AI tutor is ready day and night to give you extra practice, describe a shape in a new way, or check your drawings. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can why does this matter so much? Turns and angles are the language of direction and design. A builder in Hargeisa uses right angles to make walls that stand up straight. A traveller between Mogadishu and Kismaayo follows turns to stay on the right road. A carpenter checks square corners so that a door will close properly. And circles are everywhere too – the wheel of a cart, the rim of a cooking pot, the round anjero on the plate, the full moon that marks the start of a new month. When you understand these shapes, you understand the world around you a little better.

I can as you work, keep a pencil, some paper, and a square corner of paper beside you. Stand up when the lesson asks you to turn, and turn your whole body – this helps the idea travel from your body into your mind. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Every mathematician, builder and artist you admire once sat where you sit now, learning these very same ideas about turns, angles and circles.
I can describe movement using the words left, right and straight ahead.
I can understand and use clockwise and anticlockwise to describe turning.
I can recognise a quarter turn, a half turn, a three-quarter turn and a full turn.
I can know that four quarter turns make one full turn.
I can identify a right angle as the angle made by a quarter turn, and find right angles around you.
